WJEC CBAC
                               GCE MARKING SCHEME
                                   Predicted Paper (Advanced)

                                   ECONOMICS – UNIT 3
                                             1520U30-1


About this marking scheme
Designed in the WJEC A-Level Economics Unit 3 style. Positive marking principles apply · credit the
content learners provide rather than penalising omissions.
AOs:
 • AO1 · Knowledge of concepts/theories/models.
 • AO2 · Application to economic context.
 • AO3 · Analysis of issues and impacts.
 • AO4 · Evaluation using qualitative and quantitative evidence.


                                            (1520U30-1) Page 1
GCE A LEVEL ECONOMICS – UNIT 3
PREDICTED PAPER (ADVANCED) MARK SCHEME
Question 1 (a) · Internal economies vs diseconomies (4 marks)
AO1 – 2 marks
 • 1 mark for understanding: economies reduce average/unit cost as output rises; diseconomies raise
    average/unit cost as output rises.
 • 1 mark for accurate U-shaped LRAC diagram with output and costs axes correctly labelled. Do not
    accept 'AC' alone.
AO2 – 2 marks
 • 1 mark for TSMC internal economy of scale e.g. spreading US$380m EUV machine costs over
    billions of chips (technical/capital indivisibility); billions in fab construction costs spread over huge
    production runs.
 • 1 mark for TSMC internal diseconomy e.g. communication breakdown at Arizona fab, 30-year
    engineer burnout, managerial coordination problems across global sites, occasional yield issues.

Question 1 (b) · External economies of scale in Hsinchu (4 marks)
AO1 – 2 marks
 • 1 mark for understanding external EoS lower LRAC at all output levels due to growth of the wider
    industry/area.
 • 1 mark for a correctly drawn diagram showing LRAC shifting downward to LRAC₁.
AO2 – 2 marks
 • 1 mark for linking to case data: specialist equipment/material suppliers clustered in Hsinchu;
    government-funded ITRI; NTU postgraduate programmes; upgraded Science Park infrastructure.
 • 1 mark for linking to how LRAC falls: lower recruitment and training costs from a steady local
    pipeline of engineers; reduced logistics costs from co-located suppliers; shared R&D spillovers
    reduce innovation cost.

Question 2 (a)(b)(c) · Natural monopoly diagram (7 marks)
(a) 1 mark (AO2): unregulated profit-maximising output indicated where MC = MR.
(b) 2 marks (AO2): supernormal profit rectangle shaded as (AR − AC) × Q at the profit-maximising
output (OFR from (a)).
(c) 4 marks: why regulators use AC pricing.
 • AO1 (1 mark): knowledge that allocative efficiency requires P = MC; and that because LRAC is
    continuously falling, MC lies below AC.
 • AO2 (1 mark): recognises that MC-pricing for a natural monopoly would therefore set price below
    AC, generating losses for the firm.
 • AO3 (2 marks): developed chain · sustained losses would require permanent public subsidy (a
    fiscal burden); therefore regulators often set price at AC (average-cost pricing) which covers all
    costs including normal profit but still captures most of the potential welfare gain from monopoly
    regulation. This is a pragmatic second-best compromise between allocative efficiency and financial
    viability.

                                              (1520U30-1) Page 2
Question 3 · Evaluate Argentina's planned privatisation programme (8 marks)
         Band                    AO2 (2)                  AO3 (2)                  AO4 (4)

           3                                                               4 marks · Excellent
                                                                           evaluation, well-
                                                                           rounded judgement.

           2             2 marks · Good use       2 marks · Good           2–3 marks · Well-
                         of case data on both     analysis with            developed counter-
                         sides.                   developed chains of      argument.
                                                  reasoning.

           1             1 mark · Superficial     1 mark · Superficial     1 mark · Counter-
                         data or one-sided.       analysis.                argument present but
                                                                           not developed.

           0             0                        0                        0

In favour of success:
 • Relieves pressure on public finances · Aerolíneas has required repeated bailouts; YPF sale raises
    revenue.
 • Exposes loss-making SOEs to market discipline, potentially improving productive efficiency.
 • Vaca Muerta is a world-class shale resource that requires private FDI to develop · privatisation
    attracts capital Argentina currently lacks.
 • Milei's fiscal credibility is increasing (primary surplus achieved 2024) · may raise sale values.
 • Country risk falling (EMBI+ from 2,000 to 750 bp) · better investor confidence supports privatisation
    sales.
Against success:
 • Argentina's 1993 YPF privatisation produced disappointing investment from Repsol, prompting re-
    nationalisation · institutional memory of failure.
 • Rapid 'shock therapy' sales risk undervaluation: the peso has fallen over 96% vs US$, legal
    environment is uncertain.
 • High inflation (211% in 2023) distorts asset valuations.
 • Currency controls and high country risk deter investors; lack of deep domestic capital markets
    (similar to Angola's position).
 • Privatisation may worsen existing inequality (Gini 0.42) if shares go mainly to foreign investors and
    the wealthy.
 • Argentina's history of policy reversals (22 IMF programmes, 9 sovereign defaults) creates hold-up
    problems.
 • Foreign ownership of key natural resources could cause current-account deficits in future via profit
    repatriation.
Reversible answer.

Question 4 · Aggressive rate rises and AD in an emerging market (6 marks)
         Band                    AO1 (2)                  AO2 (2)                  AO3 (2)

           2             2 marks · Good           2 marks · Good use       2 marks · Good
                         knowledge of             of data (e.g.            analysis of impacts

                                            (1520U30-1) Page 3
                          transmission             Argentine rates            on specific AD
                          mechanisms (savers,      reaching 133%; US          components.
                          borrowers, exchange      rates at 5.5%).
                          rate).

           1              1 mark · Limited         1 mark · Limited           1 mark · Superficial
                          knowledge, one side      data reference.            analysis.
                          only.

           0              0                        0                          0

Indicative content:
 • C falls sharply: at 133% policy rates, consumer credit becomes unaffordable; high real rates crush
    durable goods purchases.
 • I collapses: business investment virtually impossible at these rates · hurdle rates are astronomical.
 • G: higher rates dramatically raise debt-service costs on Argentine dollar-denominated and peso
   debt, squeezing discretionary G.
 • X–M: high rates can attract hot money and support currency (helps stabilise against imported
    inflation), but in Argentina's case the country-risk premium dominates · capital flight continued
    despite high rates until recently.
 • Wealth effects: asset-price collapse hits net wealth and further hits C.
 • Unique EM complication: dollar-denominated debt means currency depreciation directly raises the
    peso value of debt (balance-sheet effect) · AD falls sharply through a 'sudden stop' dynamic.
 • The Argentine case shows the limits of interest-rate policy in an emerging market · at some point
    the 'side effects' dominate the direct impact.

Question 5 (a) · Monthly price index, December 2023 (3 marks)
Weighted method:
(29.7 × 27) + (32.6 × 10) + (31.7 × 14) + (13.8 × 28) + (18.9 × 8) + (8.7 × 13) / 100
= (801.9 + 326 + 443.8 + 386.4 + 151.2 + 113.1) / 100 = 2222.4 / 100 = 22.224%
Monthly price index = 122.2 (accept 122.1 – 122.3)
 • AO3 (1 mark) for use of weighted index method.
 • AO2 (2 marks) for correct answer.
 • 2 marks total for 22.2% without expressing as an index.
 • Note: this is a particularly large monthly rise reflecting the post-devaluation shock.

Question 5 (b) · Assess continued wage-price spiral in Argentina (8 marks)
         Band                    AO2 (2)                   AO3 (2)                   AO4 (4)

           3                                                                  4 marks · Excellent
                                                                              evaluation, well-
                                                                              rounded judgement.

           2              2 marks · Good data      2 marks · Good             2–3 marks · Well-
                          use on both sides,       analysis of spiral         developed counter-
                          references to at least   mechanism in               argument.
                          two Figures.             Argentina.

                                             (1520U30-1) Page 4
           1             1 mark · Data use        1 mark · Superficial    1 mark · Counter not
                         limited.                 analysis.               developed.

           0             0                        0                       0

In favour of continued spiral:
 • Argentine CPI has been above 40% every year since 2018 · deeply entrenched expectations
    (Figure 1).
 • Paritarias formally index wages to past inflation · a classic spiral mechanism.
 • Fig 2 shows monthly food inflation at 29.7% in Dec 2023 · food weight is 27%, feeding directly into
    CPI and wage demands.
 • 2024 wage agreements of 15–25% per month keep wages moving with prices.
 • Nominal wage index rose from 8 in 2019 to 210 in 2024 (Fig 3) · 26x increase · clear spiral
    dynamics.
 • Dollarised psychology · Argentines price in US$, so any peso depreciation rapidly translates into
    price rises.
Against continued spiral:
 • Real wages in Fig 3 have fallen from 148 (2019) to 96 (2024) · workers have repeatedly lost
    ground, suggesting the spiral is actually breaking.
 • Milei's fiscal adjustment has removed the monetary financing of deficits · core cause of inflation.
 • Monthly inflation fell from 25.5% (Dec 2023) to 2.7% (Oct 2024) · spiral is demonstrably
   decelerating.
 • IMF programme anchors expectations.
 • Deep recession (GDP −3.5% H1 2024) and 15% private real-wage cut reduce pressure on wages.
 • 300,000 public sector job cuts raise unemployment · weakens union bargaining power.
 • Devaluation has been front-loaded rather than trickled · if the real exchange rate stabilises,
    imported cost-push fades.
Reversible answer.


                                           (1520U30-1) Page 5
Section B · Argentina's peso crisis and Milei's chainsaw
Question 6 (a)(i) · % change in US$ price of diesel (2 marks)
(0.88 − 0.42) / 0.42 × 100 = 0.46 / 0.42 × 100 ≈ 109.5%
Accept 109% to 110%.

Question 6 (a)(ii) · % change in peso price of diesel (2 marks)
2020 peso price per litre: 0.42 × 65 = 27.3 ARS
2024 peso price per litre: 0.88 × 1,000 = 880 ARS
% change = (880 − 27.3) / 27.3 × 100 = 852.7 / 27.3 × 100 ≈ 3,124%
Accept 3,100% to 3,150%. 1 mark for correct method (multiplying US$ price by FX rate), 1 mark for
correct final answer.
(Note: case Table 1 quotes ~4,500% because it assumes a slightly different baseline FX rate and Dec
2024 FX. Both answers can earn full marks if method is correct.)

AO1 indicative:
 • MC and AC shift up (MC₂, AC₂).
 • New accurate lower output at MR = MC₂.
 • New accurate higher price (P₂).
 • New accurate lower profit area.
AO2:
 • Peso lost over 96% of US$ value since 2019 · imports priced in US$ have multiplied in peso terms.
 • Table 1 shows peso-term increases of 3,000%+ for diesel, fertiliser, gas, pharmaceuticals.
 • Argentine manufacturers laying off workers, reducing output · evidence of falling profits.

Question 7 · External vs internal factors in Argentina's 2023–24 difficulties (8
marks)

                                           (1520U30-1) Page 6
         Band                    AO2 (2)                  AO3 (2)                  AO4 (4)

           3                                                               4 marks · Well-
                                                                           rounded judgement
                                                                           recognising a mix.
                                                                           Top-band answers
                                                                           see the problems as
                                                                           primarily internal.

           2              2 marks · Data used     2 marks · Well-          2–3 marks · Well-
                          well on both sides.     developed chains of      developed counter-
                                                  reasoning.               arguments.

           1              1 mark · Data use       1 mark · Limited         1 mark · Limited
                          limited.                reasoning.               evaluation.

           0              0                       0                        0

External factors:
 • US Fed tightening raised global dollar strength and squeezed emerging markets.
 • Global commodity prices remain volatile (diesel up 110%, gas up 23% in dollar terms).
 • Rising US Treasury yields raised the cost of Argentine sovereign borrowing.
 • Regional drought affected soy harvest 2022–23, reducing export earnings.
Internal factors (likely dominant · unlike Ghana case):
 • Argentina has been serial defaulter · 9 sovereign defaults, 22 IMF programmes in 60 years · deep
    institutional failure.
 • Central bank effectively financed large fiscal deficits through money printing for decades · core
    cause of chronic inflation.
 • Complex web of capital controls, multiple exchange rates, and price controls distorted incentives.
 • Kirchnerist policies nationalised YPF (2012), scared off FDI, accumulated debt.
 • Debt service 70% of tax revenue reflects decades of internal mismanagement, not external shocks.
 • Peso fell from 38/US$ in 2019 to 1,025/US$ in 2024 · 27-fold depreciation far exceeds US$
    strengthening internationally.
 • Reforms Milei is now implementing (fiscal adjustment, deregulation) are things Argentina
    could/should have done decades ago.
 • Note the important contrast with the Ghana case: Ghana's problems were primarily externally
    triggered (US$ strength); Argentina's are primarily internal.
Reversible answer. Top-band responses will recognise that, unlike many EM crises, Argentina's 2023–
24 difficulties are widely seen as primarily self-inflicted · the opposite conclusion to the Ghana case in
the June 2024 paper.

Question 8 · Should Argentina have intervened (rates/FX) rather than
devaluing? (10 marks)
         Band                    AO2 (4)                  AO3 (3)                  AO4 (3)


                                            (1520U30-1) Page 7
           3             4 marks · Data           3 marks · Excellent      3 marks · Well-
                         embedded, both           analysis of both rates   rounded judgement
                         intervention options     AND FX intervention.     with reference to both
                         addressed.                                        forms.

           2             2–3 marks · Case         2 marks · Developed      2 marks · Well-
                         used well, may focus     analysis, narrower.      developed counter.
                         on one.

           1             1 mark · Limited         1 mark · Limited         1 mark · Limited
                         use.                     analysis.                evaluation.

           0             0                        0                        0

Support intervention (continue raising rates / continue FX intervention):
 • Peso had depreciated over 96% since 2019 · stabilisation necessary to stop the rot.
 • Further rate hikes could have supported the peso and made it more attractive to hold.
 • Prior Kirchnerist approach of 'cepo cambiario' (capital controls + FX intervention) was the
    established orthodoxy.
 • Protects dollar-debt holders from balance-sheet losses that devaluation causes.
 • Devaluation inflicts severe short-term pain (poverty rate rose to 55% · Table 2).
Against intervention / in favour of Milei's devaluation:
 • Reserves were actually negative (−$11bn, Nov 2023, Fig 2) · FX intervention was literally
    impossible without new dollars.
 • Interest rates already at 133% (Q4 data) · further rises would have crushed real economy without
    supporting the peso.
 • Parallel 'blue dollar' rate showed the official rate had become fictional; continued defence would
    waste remaining resources.
 • Deep structural causes (fiscal deficit, money printing) require fiscal and monetary reform · FX/rate
    intervention alone treats symptoms.
 • Orthodox devaluation + fiscal adjustment has produced results: inflation down from 25.5% to 2.7%
   monthly, primary surplus achieved, reserves rebuilt to +$6.5bn (Table 2).
 • Global experience: countries that have ended chronic high inflation (Israel 1985, Brazil 1994) all
   used devaluation + fiscal reform, not continued FX support.
Reversible answer. If only one intervention is evaluated, maximum Band 2 (7/10).

Question 9 · Will Milei's shock therapy + IMF improve living standards? (12
marks)
         Band                    AO2 (4)                  AO3 (3)                 AO4 (5)

           3             4 marks · Case           3 marks · Excellent      5 marks · Well-
                         embedded.                analysis focused on      rounded judgement.
                                                  LS indicators.

           2             2–3 marks · Case         2 marks · Developed      3–4 marks · Well-
                         used well but uneven.    reasoning.               developed counter-
                                                                           arguments.

           1             1 mark · Superficial     1 mark · Limited.        1–2 marks · Limited.

                                            (1520U30-1) Page 8
                         case use.

           0             0                        0                        0

In favour of improved LS:
 • Falling inflation is itself an LS improvement · especially for poor, who cannot hedge via US$
    accounts.
 • Monthly inflation collapse (25.5% → 2.7%) means real incomes stabilising.
 • Return of primary fiscal surplus allows future investment in health/education (Table 2 shows LS
    already relatively high on most dimensions).
 • Falling country risk (2,000 → 750 bp) supports future investment and jobs.
 • Q3 2024 GDP grew 3.9% QoQ · signs of recovery.
 • Ends decades of monetary financing that caused chronic inflation tax on the poor.
 • IMF programme provides hard-currency buffer, reducing balance-of-payments risk.
 • Vaca Muerta energy exports could generate sustained FX earnings.
Against:
 • Poverty rate rose from 40% to 55% (Table 2) · severe short-term damage.
 • Real wages in private sector fell 15% in Q1 2024 · catastrophic for workers.
 • GDP shrank 3.5% YoY in H1 2024 · deep recession.
 • 300,000 public-sector jobs cut · immediate unemployment impact.
 • 1990s 'Convertibility' ended in 2001–02 collapse · similar shock therapy failed before.
 • Inequality (Gini 0.42) may rise as adjustment falls on wages/public sector.
 • IMF has 22 previous Argentine programmes · poor track record of sustained success.
 • Food banks overwhelmed · immediate human cost.
 • Milei's political support depends on results; if recovery stalls, reforms may be reversed (Argentina's
   pattern).
 • Proposed dollarisation would lock in current low real wages, making future adjustment impossible.
Band 3 requires a clear focus on living-standard indicators and IMF impact.
